The System Had Been Slow for Three Weeks. Every Engineer on the Team Had a Different Theory. All of Them Were Wrong.

The problem with smart people in a room is that smart people have theories.

7 min read
Just now

--

The Slowdown That Nobody Could Explain
It started on a Tuesday. Not dramatically. Not with an alert that woke anyone up or a dashboard that turned red or a customer escalation that landed in the engineering channel. It started the way the most expensive performance problems start, which is quietly and gradually and in a way that is easy to attribute to noise for long enough that by the time you stop attributing it to noise it has been happening for three weeks.
Our order search API had been returning results in 340 milliseconds at p99 for the previous four months. Consistent, predictable, within the bounds of what we had designed for. Over three weeks in November the p99 climbed to 890 milliseconds. Not a spike. A slope. Eleven milliseconds per day on average, invisible on any single day, visible only when you looked at the thirty-day chart and saw the line moving in one direction without interruption.
By the time I pulled the thirty-day chart we had been slow for three weeks and had four engineers with four different theories about why.
The Theories
Nadia said it was the database. She had been watching query execution times and had noticed that the primary search query had gone from 45 milliseconds median to 71 milliseconds median over the same period. She said the index was degrading under write load and needed a rebuild.
James said it was the cache. He had pulled cache hit rates and seen them drop from 78 percent to 61 percent over three weeks. He said the TTL configuration was wrong for the current traffic pattern and the increased cache misses were putting load on the database that was showing up as query degradation.
Prerna said it was the application layer. She had profiled the search service and found that object serialization in the response pipeline was taking 40 percent longer than it had four months ago. She said a dependency update three weeks earlier had introduced a regression in the serialization library.
Vikram said it was infrastructure. He had noticed that the search service had been migrated to a new node class in the infrastructure update that had happened three weeks and two days before the slowdown started. He said the new node class had different memory characteristics and the JVM was garbage collecting more frequently under the same load.
Four engineers. Four theories. All of them grounded in real data. All of them pointing at a real degradation in the component they were watching. None of them able to explain why their component had degraded at the same time and at the same rate.
That convergence was the signal nobody was reading.
What the Convergence Was Actually Saying
I had been in enough performance investigations to know that when multiple components degrade simultaneously at the same rate, the degradation is almost never originating in any of those components. Simultaneous multi-component degradation has one common cause: something upstream of all of them changed, and the change is propagating through the system in a way that looks like independent degradation in each component that is affected by it.
The database query was slower because it was doing more work. The cache hit rate was lower because something was generating more unique cache keys than it had before, producing more misses against the same cache configuration. The serialization was slower because the responses were larger. The garbage collection was more frequent because the heap was carrying more data per request.
Everything was slower and larger because the requests themselves had become larger or more numerous or both, and none of the four engineers watching individual components had been looking at the requests.
I pulled the request analysis. In three weeks our average search query had gone from returning 23 results to returning 47 results. Not because search traffic had changed. Because a product change three weeks earlier had modified the default result set size from 20 to 50 for a specific user cohort that turned out to represent 40 percent of our search volume.
The product change had been documented. It had been deployed. It had not been connected to the performance monitoring because nobody had mapped the relationship between result set size and the downstream resource consumption of every component in the search pipeline.
Every engineer on the team had been correct about their component. Nobody had been looking at what was feeding all of them.
What Siloed Diagnosis Costs
The three weeks we spent with four engineers investigating four components in parallel was not wasted work. Each investigation had produced accurate information about the component it examined. The information was just insufficient to explain the system behavior because system behavior is not the sum of component behaviors. It is the product of component interactions, and component interactions are invisible when the investigation is organized by component ownership rather than by causal chain.
We had organized the investigation the way engineering teams almost always organize performance investigations, which is by assigning the problem to the engineers who own the components that are exhibiting symptoms. That organization is efficient for problems that originate in a single component. It is systematically inefficient for problems that originate upstream of multiple components because it produces parallel investigations that each find real degradation and none of which can explain why the degradation is happening across components simultaneously.
The investigation structure that would have found the result set change in the first week rather than the third week starts from the symptom at the system boundary rather than from the symptoms inside the system. What changed about the requests this service is receiving. What changed about the responses it is producing. What changed about the data flowing through it at the top of the pipeline before it reaches any of the components that are now behaving differently.
That question points at the feed before it points at the components. And the feed is where the answer was.
The Fix That Took Four Hours
Once we had identified the result set size change the fix was straightforward. A configuration update that applied pagination differently to the affected cohort, reducing the default result set from 50 to 25 with an explicit load-more mechanism for users who needed more results in a single query.
The fix took four hours of engineering work. The investigation that preceded it took three weeks and four engineers working in parallel.
The ratio of investigation time to fix time is the number that should bother every engineering team that runs performance reviews. When investigation takes twenty times longer than the fix it produces, the investigation methodology is the problem. Not the engineers. Not the components they were watching. The structure that organized the investigation around component ownership rather than causal chain.
What Changed About How We Investigate
The investigation framework I changed after that November is organized around a single first question that precedes all component-level analysis.
What changed in the system in the period before the degradation began.
Not what component is degrading. Not which metric is moving. What changed. Deployments, configuration updates, product changes, infrastructure migrations, dependency updates, traffic pattern shifts, data volume changes. Everything that is different about the system in the degraded period compared to the period before it.
That question takes twenty minutes to answer thoroughly when the change tracking is maintained correctly. It takes longer when it is not. The twenty minutes almost always narrows the investigation from four parallel component analyses to one or two targeted examinations of the changes that could plausibly produce the observed symptom pattern.
The result set change would have appeared in that twenty-minute review. It had been deployed three weeks and two days before the slowdown started and it had modified the data volume flowing through every component in the search pipeline. It was in the deployment log. Nobody had looked at the deployment log because the investigation had started with the components rather than with what had changed before the components started behaving differently.
The Performance Investigation That Finds the Cause Instead of the Symptoms
The causal chain analysis that starts from system boundary changes rather than component symptoms, the request and response profiling that surfaces upstream changes before the component investigation begins, the change tracking practices that make the twenty-minute review possible when the degradation starts, and the p99 diagnostic sequence that distinguishes gradual regression from spike failures and points the investigation at the right category of cause that is inside The Senior Backend Engineer Handbook.
Because the performance investigations that cost the most are not the ones where the engineers looking at the components are wrong. They are the ones where every engineer is right about their component and nobody is looking at what changed upstream of all of them.
Nadia was right about the database. James was right about the cache. Prerna was right about the serialization. Vikram was right about the garbage collection.
The result set had doubled. Nobody had looked at the result set for three weeks because the investigation had been organized around who owned what rather than around what had changed and when.
Four engineers. Three weeks. One product configuration change that had been in the deployment log the entire time.
The longer performance investigation breakdowns go out on Substack.
